name: Santiago del Estero (Argentina)

colors.symbolism: the colours of the Argentine national flag
the colours of the Argentine national flag
the federalist red of Santiago del Estero, the oldest continuously inhabited Spanish settlement in Argentina and a federalist stronghold
the Inca Sun and the colour of the May sun

description: Five vertical stripes of light blue, white, red, white and light blue (proportions 2:1:4:1:2); centred on the broad red middle stripe, a yellow stylised sun on which is set a red silhouette of a Quichua chacarera dancer.
history: Adopted by provincial law 6.530 on 24 March 1985, the flag was the result of a 1985 contest organised by the provincial government and won by Eduardo Felipe Mansilla. The colours combine the celestial and white of the Argentine national flag with the federalist red of caudillo Juan Felipe Ibarra (who governed Santiago del Estero between 1820 and 1851); the central emblem honours the chacarera dance and the surviving Quichua culture of the province.

features.symbolism: the light-blue and white of the Argentine national flag flanking a central red field — the federalist red of Juan Felipe Ibarra and the long federalist tradition of Santiago del Estero
shared with the national flag of Argentina; the irradiating sun was an Andean and Inca solar emblem long before the May Revolution
represents the chacarera — the folk dance born in Santiago del Estero — and the Quichua-speaking culture of the province, where Quichua is still spoken alongside Spanish
